name: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries

governance.decision_making: Unanimous agreement required for quota changes; Saudi Arabia holds de facto veto through swing producer status

strategic_priorities: Defending oil prices above member fiscal breakeven levels through coordinated production cuts
Managing OPEC+ cohesion as Russia's war in Ukraine strains alliance with key non-OPEC members
Countering narrative of peak oil demand while diversifying revenues domestically
Preserving market share against US shale, Canadian oil sands, and Brazilian pre-salt competition
Positioning Gulf members as long-term suppliers in a decarbonizing world through LNG and hydrogen exports
Navigating Iran's return to market after any potential nuclear deal
summary: OPEC, operating as the broader OPEC+ coalition with Russia and other partners, controls roughly 40% of global oil production and 80% of proven reserves, giving it outsized influence over global energy prices. The group has pursued an aggressive production-cut strategy since 2022 to support prices, but faces persistent headwinds from strong US shale growth, weakening Chinese demand, and the long-term energy transition. Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 fiscal needs create a structural tension between high prices and market share protection.
recent_developments: OPEC+ extended voluntary production cuts of 2.2 million barrels per day through Q2 2026, as Brent crude struggled to hold above $75 despite cuts
Saudi Arabia signaled it would not indefinitely absorb outsized cut burdens; warned members with poor quota compliance — notably Iraq and UAE — to reduce overproduction
US crude production reached a new record of 13.6 million barrels per day in late 2025, limiting OPEC+ ability to tighten the market
Iran's production recovered to approximately 3.3 million barrels per day, with much of the increase absorbed by China at discounted prices, undermining cartel discipline
Venezuela's production inched up to 900,000 barrels per day following partial US sanctions relief, adding further supply pressure
risks: Brent price below $75 for a sustained period would stress the fiscal positions of all Gulf members
OPEC+ cohesion fragile: Iraq, UAE, and Kazakhstan chronically overproduce against quotas
Accelerating energy transition in Europe and China reduces long-term demand outlook
Geopolitical risk: any direct Iran-Israel or Iran-US escalation could disrupt Strait of Hormuz — 20% of global oil trade
